data.title: Las Vegas Sands: Singapore Is Not Enough
data.description:
Summary

Las Vegas Sands (LVS) is one of the premier integrated resorts globally with a heavy presence in the core Asian gaming markets of Macau and Singapore. Our main concern with the stock is a potential slowdown in Macau gaming growth coupled with long-term margin compression from excess competition in Macau as new competitor capacity comes online in 2026-2027. While the Singapore gaming market remains robust, without significant margin and EBITDA improvement in Macau, we believe the stock has more downside from current levels.

All integrated resorts are in Asia

LVS EBITDA is derived from Singapore and Macau, as the company no longer has any assets in the United States. LVS operates Marina Bay Sands ((MBS) in Singapore and operates Sands Macau, Venetian, Londoner, Parisian, and The Plaza in Macau. LVS completed the sale of its Las Vegas resort assets in February 2022 to private equity group Apollo and VICI Properties.

Singapore > Macau in terms of EBITDA

As a percentage of adjusted property EBITDA in 2025, Singapore was roughly 56% and Macau was 44% of LVS' $5.2bn EBITDA whereas in 2023-2024, Macau was the majority of group EBITDA at roughly 54%.

Not only is Singapore a greater percentage of EBITDA, but adjusted property EBITDA margins are also higher in Singapore, with 4Q25 margins reaching 50.3% (+3.1% yoy) while Macau EBITDA margins fell to 29.5% (-2.7% yoy).

Margins are higher in Singapore as gaming tax rates are lower when compared to Macau (Singapore at 18-22% compared to Macau at 40%). In addition, Singapore only has two integrated resorts split between two gaming operators, while Macau has twenty casinos spread among six operators. As a result, gaming operators in Macau face stiffer competition to gain customers and recently have spent more in terms of marketing expenses to attract players.

Not in the right segment yet

The premium mass gaming segment has been driving the majority of the GGR growth recently in Macau. However, LVS' product offering in Macau is geared more towards the mid- to lower spending player, which has not seen a significant recovery yet. In terms of product offering, LVS is disadvantaged vs. competitors such as Wynn and MGM, who traditionally have targeted the higher end segment through more premium service along with higher end properties that cater to the more affluent player.

Intense competition in Macau

We expect the competition for player acquisition to remain intense in Macau in the near-term as new gaming capacity continues to come online. LVS' largest competitor in Macau, Galaxy Entertainment, launched the ultra-high end Capella Hotel in February. Galaxy will also launch Phase 4 of its main integrated resort in addition to completing renovation of its StarWorld property in 2027. Melco Resorts is also expected to open its new hotel REM, formerly The Countdown, in 2026. Other gaming operators such as Wynn, LVS, SJM, and MGM will mainly focus on internal refurbishments and along with continued ramp up of existing properties, compete with the new capacity.

We expect player reinvestment costs to remain high as operators with new capacity need to draw in new customers, while operators without new capacity need to reinvest in order to keep their current client base from leaving. Hence, we do not expect any sustainable increase in EBITDA margins or EBITDA in the near term for LVS. In addition, new capacity is likely to lead to market share losses for LVS, which was the case in 2025, further pressuring profit growth.

Intense competition in regionally

Longer term, not only do we see increased capacity in Macau, but regional integrated resorts are likely to come online starting in 2030 with MGM Osaka. In addition, the Japanese government will open another bidding process in 2027 for a second gaming site. Furthermore, Wynn Al Marjan Island in UAE is expected to open in 2027, coupled with new projects in Manila and Cambodia from 2026-2029.

Gaming growth slowing in Macau

Total GGR (gross gaming revenue) for Macau grew by 9% in 2024 vs. 24% in 2023. YTD through March, GGR is +14%. Current consensus estimates for GGR growth in 2026 are around mid-single digits. In 2025, Macau's GGR per visitor fell by 5% yoy. One of the main reasons is because Macau has continued to build out its non-gaming infrastructure in the forms of arenas to host concerts and convention halls for MICE events. Hence, the type of customer coming to Macau may not be the heavy gambler but now instead is more of the leisurely customer who does not gamble.

Macro issues hurt GGR

We expect gaming revenues to see slower growth rates in 2026 due to geopolitical risks leading to greater consumer uncertainty and slower economic growth from rising oil prices. In addition, prolonged economic slowdown in China, where the Chinese government forecasted GDP growth of 4.5% vs. 5% in 2025, coupled with a weak property market, slowing consumer consumption, and tariff uncertainty, are all likely to rein in consumer spending in 2026, which could further hurt gaming revenues.

Singapore is strong but not enough

MBS is seeing robust growth due to strong international tourism into Singapore, which helped to push Singapore GGR by 24% in 2025 (vs. 8% in 2024). In addition, LVS has shifted its customer focus at MBS to a more premium base, which results in greater operating leverage for earnings. In addition, LVS has captured higher GGR market share in Singapore against its main competitor, Resorts World, due to having a better product offering catering to the premium customer segment.

LVS will also build its fourth tower at MBS, which will feature a 55 story luxury building coupled with a 15,000 seat arena, MICE space, along with additional gaming and retail areas. The expected opening date is early 2031.

Despite strong Singapore growth, LVS' share price fell by as much as 13% after the company reported 4Q25 results in late January 2026 due to weakness in Macau. Hence, we believe Macau is still a major component of share price sentiment.

Lack of catalysts

We suspect that the promotional activity remains high for Macau player acquisition and hence expect more downside than upside surprise for LVS shares. LVS' quarterly dividend has recently only been around 1/3 of its pre-pandemic levels. On a forward EV/EBITDA basis, LVS is trading around 10.5-11x compared to its historical average of 13x, and while the valuation remains cheaper from a historical perspective, we would remain cautious given questionable growth prospects in Macau.

Upside risks

Upside risks to our view include stronger than expected EBITDA recovery in Macau from market share gains or lower reinvestment rates. Also, if Macau GGR surprises on the upside, the entire universe of Macau gaming stocks tends to react positively.

Conclusion

We would be sellers of the stock at these levels given LVS' lack of new Macau capacity, its premium product offering being weaker compared to peers, a potential slowdown in Macau gaming growth, coupled with both short- and long term competitor capacity expansion leading to higher marketing costs, lower market share, and earnings compression in Macau.

data.title: Albertsons: Private Labels And Data Drive The Long-Term Edge Despite Opioid Headwinds
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Albertsons Companies (ACI), I highlighted how the “Failed Kroger Deal Fallout Creates An Attractive Entry Point,” rating them a Buy thanks to their robust cash flow, digital and pharma expansion, and significant cost-savings program helping them navigate a rough environment.

Following the opioid settlement news overshadowing the strong dividend increase and renewed buybacks alongside higher investments, ACI continues to trade at an attractive valuation that already seems to take into account several risks.

Business Transformation Continues

ACI’s latest report was solid overall, with a beat on EPS despite a slight miss on revenue, reporting a 2% increase in identical sales and a strong 21% jump in digital sales in 2025, as well as a massive $773.8 million accounting loss related to the Opioid Settlement Framework they announced on the day of the release, expected to be completed over the following 9 years.

Meanwhile, the free cash flow dropped to $527.3 million in FY25 compared to $749.4 million in FY24 despite a drop in CAPEX, with a big impact coming from negative working capital change related to inventories, which doesn't indicate a major deterioration in the business.

For FY26, ACI expects identical sales to be 0% to 1% higher (150 bp headwind from the Inflation Reduction Act's Medicare Drug Price Negotiation Program), with $3.85 billion to $3.925 billion in Adj. EBITDA and $2 billion to $2.2 billion in CAPEX. The latter is significantly above the ~$1.84 billion spent in FY25, backing their digital/e-commerce infrastructure and remodeling stores while also aiming to automate their supply chain, which is part of their $1.5 billion productivity savings 3-year target announced over a year ago.

Financially, based on ACI’s latest report, we continue to see an overall normal position for a company in this industry, with current assets below their current liabilities and growing but still manageable debt.

Recently, ACI also priced a $2.1 billion senior notes offering, with $1.2 billion due in 2032 coming at 5.625% and $900 million due in 2034 at 5.75%. This is used to redeem their $1.35 billion 4.625% notes due 2027 and $750 million 5.875% notes due 2028, pushing their maturities to a much more comfortable spot while also increasing their interest expenses by roughly $12.75 million per year.

During this report, Albertsons also announced a major 13.3% increase in their dividend, bringing it to $0.17 per share quarterly, for a yield of about 4% now, while also upsizing their buyback authorization to $2 billion after spending a strong $1.49 billion in FY25, for a combined $1.81 billion, meaning a combined yield of 20.9% in FY25, thanks especially to their accelerated buyback agreement for $750 million - but very strong even without it.

Iran Conflict Elevates the Risk

With mixed signals regarding the Iran conflict following the US blockade announcement after failed peace negotiations but some renewed optimism a day after thanks to renewed ceasefire talks, the risk of significant pressure on the environment rises with every extra day of significant war premiums on these core commodities.

At this point, it looks like we’re looking at much more than a “temporary” increase in pump prices, as even if we see an immediate peace today, the region’s energy infrastructure has suffered significant damage that the market may not be able to price in just yet, with Qatar even stating back over a month ago (around March 19) that the damage done to their Ras Laffan Industrial City following Iran’s retaliation could take between 3 and 5 years to repair.

This is just one piece of the puzzle, however, as we’re talking about spikes in vital commodities at a time when the global economy was starting to show signs of a recovery after the rate hikes following the pandemic, while also hurting the planting season significantly, potentially leading to significant issues in the near term.

As highlighted before, this can put the Fed and other central banks in a tough position as well, as they may be forced to pause rate cuts or even consider hiking if the inflationary wave is too big, which can put even more pressure on an already weak consumer environment and valuations across the board.

Meanwhile, although this weak consumer environment is also an advantage for companies like ACI (will detail this below), discretionary budgets can continue to be affected by such moves, especially if companies continue to pass these higher costs on to the consumer and borrowing costs rise, with the previous rate hikes resulting in significant trade-downs, high levels of stress, and even the essentials like groceries seeing softened spending intentions, based on a recent Deloitte report.

This is something that goes well beyond supply chain issues or tariffs, as a higher-for-longer rate environment can result in higher borrowing costs and more pressure on ACI following each refinancing, and we can see their $2.1 billion notes offering coming at higher rates than the ones they refinanced, although they won’t have to do it anymore for a while.

Even if ACI’s debt was significantly smaller, this can still affect the company from several points of view, as we’re talking about the potential to see smaller discretionary budgets for their consumers, potentially worsening the K-shaped economy and affecting the company’s sales.

The Retailer Advantage

Back in January, Morgan Stanley saw ACI as being the “most at risk in grocery wars” given their reliance on pharma sales for growth compared to peers, citing the impact from the Inflation Reduction Act and slowing GLP-1 sales - although the latter also translates into less pressure on retail spending.

However, the same institution recently released its latest expectations for GLP-1 adoption, expecting usage to reach roughly 15% of the American population by 2035, which can be a massive tailwind for their pharma sales.

At the same time, Deutsche Bank shared a very similar warning to mine regarding the consumer-packaged goods landscape, questioning the defensiveness of legacy brands in the face of the rapidly evolving retail environment. This also comes with a major tailwind for ACI, however, as private label brands can offer significant potential from several points of view.

First of all, this offers significant power of negotiation against legacy brands and especially smaller producers given the artificially lower shelf space, while private labels don’t need marketing and are basically almost the same as cutting out the middleman, while the company is targeting significant growth in private labels to as much as 30% of total sales, as highlighted previously, allowing them to capitalize on the consumer’s trade-downs.

Not only that, but these brands also offer differentiation from peers, as they’re not just another store where you find mostly the same products from the same legacy brands anymore, posing a transformative point for the industry as a whole.

On top of that, thanks to all the data gathered from these trade-downs while trust in their own brands rises, the company can further tailor their portfolio to offer what consumers want - especially if we think about their plans for digital expansion that can take this a step further while gathering important data - allowing them to also “capture” the consumer during a potential recovery.

Risks Reiterated

Although there are certain advantages when talking about retailers compared to legacy brands, the industry as a whole remains very competitive, with several other local or even European players wanting to take advantage of the current consumer trade-downs through their own private label brands.

Meanwhile, given the ongoing Iran conflict and especially the potential pressure it can come with, there is certainly room for the environment to get even worse, which can also hurt ACI and its peers.

Valuation

For ACI’s DCF to Equity valuation, given their CAPEX guidance and assuming a pivotal year, I’ll assume a starting FCF of $500 million in 2026, without taking into account any changes in net borrowings since the company’s balance sheet allows it, especially after the recent refinancing.

Assuming a normalization in CAPEX in FY27, I’ll assume this can jump to FY24’s $750 million thanks to their supply chain optimizations, cost savings, buybacks boosting the final per share result, and a potential macro recovery alongside their private label expansion offsetting the hit from the opioid settlements, which would be followed by a CAGR of 6.5% until FY30 (similar to the market's EPS estimates), with a 4% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a 10% discount rate to demand a strong margin of safety given the environment, with 509.7 million diluted shares outstanding as of their Q4’FY25 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $11.01 billion, for an intrinsic value of $21.60 per share, which is above the current $16.12 at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

ACI is rated Buy, backed by an attractive valuation and strong foundation that allows them to deliver very strong shareholder returns, standing to benefit from current and future trends alongside their digital expansion.

There are risks to remember given the fact that not even this industry is immune to the Iran conflict and significant competitive pressure, but the current valuation seems to already take into account significant levels of risk
